title: Durable goods inflation and effective tariffs
gist: US durable goods prices (appliances, electronics, furniture) fell as much as 3% year-over-year through early 2025 while effective tariffs sat near 2.5%; in 2025 the effective tariff rate more than quadrupled to over 11% and durable goods deflation reversed to 2-3% inflation by early 2026, suggesting import taxes helped end the deflation.

insights.claim: US durable goods prices such as appliances, electronics, and furniture were in deflation from mid-2023 through early 2025, declining by as much as 3% year-over-year while effective tariffs held near 2.5%.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Establishes a multi-year disinflationary baseline for consumer durables that a tariff shock could disrupt — a benchmark for gauging how much of recent goods inflation is policy-driven.
insights.quote: Deflation was the norm from mid-2023 through early 2025, with prices declining by as much as 3% year-over-year.
insights.locator: Dramatic shifts in durable goods prices and trade policy

insights.claim: The effective tariff rate is defined as total tariff revenue collected by the government divided by the total value of all imported goods.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Gives investors a replicable, revenue-based measure of actual tariff burden rather than headline statutory rates, useful for tracking real trade-policy pressure on prices.
insights.quote: the effective tariff rate is total tariff revenue collected by the government divided by the total value of all imported goods
insights.locator: Dramatic shifts in durable goods prices and trade policy

insights.claim: In 2025 the US effective tariff rate surged from roughly 2.5% to over 11%, more than quadrupling in about a year.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: A more-than-4x jump in the realized tariff burden is a material cost shock to import-dependent supply chains and a key macro variable to size against goods-price inflation.
insights.quote: The effective tariff rate surged from roughly 2.5% to over 11%, more than quadrupling in about a year.

insights.claim: Shortly after the 2025 tariff surge, durable goods prices reversed from declines to increases of around 2% to 3% year-over-year by early 2026, with import taxes suggested as a factor ending the prior deflation.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Ties consumer durables inflation directly to trade policy — a signal that tariff changes, not just demand, are moving goods CPI and margins for importers and retailers.
insights.quote: the coinciding tariff spike and price acceleration suggest import taxes may be playing a role in ending the deflation consumers experienced with durable goods

insights.claim: Durable goods prices reflect many factors beyond import tariffs, so the tariff-inflation relationship is complex despite the striking timing.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: A caution against over-attributing goods inflation to tariffs alone — investors should weigh demand, FX, and supply factors before pricing a pure tariff pass-through.

insights.claim: Effective tariffs appear to have peaked in late 2025 and have since begun declining, which if continued could moderate durable goods inflation and potentially return prices to declines.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: A falling effective tariff rate is a leading indicator that goods disinflation may resume — worth monitoring for the durable goods component of CPI/PCE and rate-path implications.
insights.quote: If this trend continues, we could see a corresponding moderation in durable goods inflation and potentially even a return to the price declines that characterized the earlier period.
